hook: An alcoholic drink with caffeine and energy drink ingredients that regulators forced off shelves in 2008, years before Four Loko went through the same thing.
fate: The original caffeinated Tilt formula was pulled in 2008 after Anheuser-Busch agreed with state attorneys general to stop making caffeinated alcoholic drinks. The brand continued for a while in a changed version without the stimulant ingredients.
summary: Tilt launched in 2005 as an alcoholic drink from Anheuser-Busch that mixed malt alcohol with caffeine, guarana, and ginseng. It was sold in bright cans that looked like energy drinks. In 2008, after pressure from state regulators, Anheuser-Busch agreed to stop making caffeinated alcoholic drinks and reformulated Tilt without the stimulant ingredients. The original formula was gone. A changed version of Tilt continued for a few more years.

seo.meta_title: What Happened to Tilt? The Original Caffeinated Alcohol Drink
seo.meta_description: Tilt was Anheuser-Busch's alcoholic energy drink. Learn why the original caffeinated formula disappeared in 2008 and how later Tilt versions changed.
keywords: pre Four Loko alcoholic energy drink

notable_products: Tilt Original, the caffeinated version that mixed malt alcohol with caffeine, guarana, and ginseng
Tilt Red, a flavor variation of the original
Reformulated Tilt, a later version sold without caffeine or stimulant ingredients after 2008
verified_facts: Tilt launched in 2005 as an Anheuser-Busch alcoholic energy drink.
The original formula included caffeine, guarana, and ginseng.
In 2008, Anheuser-Busch agreed with multiple state attorneys general to stop producing caffeinated alcoholic beverages and reformulate Tilt.
The original caffeinated version of Tilt ended in 2008.
A later non-original Tilt continued in reformulated form and was adjusted again in 2011.
cultural_memory: Tilt is remembered as one of the drinks that made caffeinated alcohol feel like a mainstream packaged category instead of a niche experiment.
Its branding looked more like an energy drink than a traditional beer product, which was part of its appeal and part of the controversy.
For a lot of people, Tilt sits in memory as part of the same era that later made Four Loko a national flashpoint.
The original drink felt like it was selling two promises at once: intoxication and energy.
overview_md:
**Tilt** was an alcoholic drink that came out in 2005 from Anheuser-Busch. The original version mixed malt alcohol with caffeine, guarana, and ginseng. It came in bright cans that looked more like an energy drink than a beer, which was a big part of its appeal.

At the time, Tilt was part of a small group of drinks trying to combine alcohol with energy drink ingredients. Sparks was another one. The idea was that you could drink alcohol and still feel alert. That pitch worked on a lot of younger drinkers who picked up the cans at convenience stores and gas stations.

But health researchers and state regulators started raising concerns. They argued that mixing caffeine with alcohol could make people feel more alert than they really were, which could lead to drinking more and making riskier decisions. The combination of stimulants and alcohol in a single brightly colored can also drew criticism for how it was marketed.

By 2008 the pressure had turned into formal action. California and a group of other states pushed Anheuser-Busch to stop making caffeinated alcoholic drinks. In June 2008, Anheuser-Busch agreed. The company stopped producing Tilt in its original form and reformulated it without caffeine or other stimulant ingredients.

The original Tilt was gone. A changed version of the brand kept going for a few more years before fading out entirely.

Tilt came before Four Loko became the most famous name in caffeinated alcohol. A lot of people who remember that whole era of drinks think of Tilt as the early version of the same story that Four Loko made much bigger a few years later.

timeline.text: Tilt launches from Anheuser-Busch. The original formula includes alcohol plus caffeine, guarana, and ginseng. It is sold in bright cans that look like energy drinks.

California and other states push Anheuser-Busch to stop making caffeinated alcoholic drinks. In June 2008 the company agrees, reformulating Tilt without stimulant ingredients. The original formula is gone.
Later versions of Tilt continue in changed form with lower alcohol content before the brand fades out.

faq.question: Was Tilt banned?
faq.answer: The original caffeinated version was ended in 2008 through a multistate agreement. Anheuser-Busch agreed to stop making caffeinated alcoholic drinks, so the classic formula disappeared. The brand name continued for a while in a changed form without the stimulant ingredients.

faq.question: What was in original Tilt?
faq.answer: The original version included malt alcohol plus stimulant ingredients: caffeine, guarana, and ginseng.

faq.question: Why did original Tilt go away?
faq.answer: State regulators pushed back on caffeinated alcohol and Anheuser-Busch agreed in 2008 to stop producing those kinds of drinks. The company reformulated Tilt to remove the caffeine and other stimulants.

faq.question: Did Tilt come before Four Loko?
faq.answer: Yes. Tilt was already on the market years before Four Loko became the most famous name in caffeinated alcohol.

faq.question: Did Tilt completely disappear in 2008?
faq.answer: The original caffeinated formula did. Later versions of Tilt kept going for a few more years in a changed form without the stimulant ingredients.
